lines.greek: ἄνδρα μοι ἔννεπε, μοῦσα, πολύτροπον, ὃς μάλα πολλὰ
πλάγχθη, ἐπεὶ Τροίης ἱερὸν πτολίεθρον ἔπερσεν·
πολλῶν δʼ ἀνθρώπων ἴδεν ἄστεα καὶ νόον ἔγνω,
πολλὰ δʼ ὅ γʼ ἐν πόντῳ πάθεν ἄλγεα ὃν κατὰ θυμόν,
ἀρνύμενος ἥν τε ψυχὴν καὶ νόστον ἑταίρων.
ἀλλʼ οὐδʼ ὣς ἑτάρους ἐρρύσατο, ἱέμενός περ·
αὐτῶν γὰρ σφετέρῃσιν ἀτασθαλίῃσιν ὄλοντο,
νήπιοι, οἳ κατὰ βοῦς Ὑπερίονος Ἠελίοιο
ἤσθιον· αὐτὰρ ὁ τοῖσιν ἀφείλετο νόστιμον ἦμαρ.
τῶν ἁμόθεν γε, θεά, θύγατερ Διός, εἰπὲ καὶ ἡμῖν.
ἔνθʼ ἄλλοι μὲν πάντες, ὅσοι φύγον αἰπὺν ὄλεθρον,
οἴκοι ἔσαν, πόλεμόν τε πεφευγότες ἠδὲ θάλασσαν·
τὸν δʼ οἶον νόστου κεχρημένον ἠδὲ γυναικὸς
νύμφη πότνιʼ ἔρυκε Καλυψὼ δῖα θεάων
ἐν σπέσσι γλαφυροῖσι, λιλαιομένη πόσιν εἶναι.
ἀλλʼ ὅτε δὴ ἔτος ἦλθε περιπλομένων ἐνιαυτῶν,
τῷ οἱ ἐπεκλώσαντο θεοὶ οἶκόνδε νέεσθαι
εἰς Ἰθάκην, οὐδʼ ἔνθα πεφυγμένος ἦεν ἀέθλων
καὶ μετὰ οἷσι φίλοισι. θεοὶ δʼ ἐλέαιρον ἅπαντες
νόσφι Ποσειδάωνος· ὁ δʼ ἀσπερχὲς μενέαινεν

interlinear: The-man to-me tell-of, Muse, the-much-turned, who very many
ways was-driven-wandering, after of-Troy the-sacred citadel he-sacked;
of-many men he-saw the-cities and the-mind he-came-to-know,
many too in the-sea he-suffered griefs in his heart,
striving-for his-own life and the-homecoming of-his-comrades.
But not-even so his-comrades he-saved, desiring-it though-he-was;
by-their-own for recklessnesses they-perished,
fools, who the-cattle of-Hyperion the-Sun
were-devouring; but-he from-them took-away the-homecoming day.
Of-these-things from-some-point, goddess, daughter of-Zeus, tell also to-us.
Then the-others indeed all, as-many-as had-fled sheer destruction,
at-home were, the-war having-escaped and the-sea;
him but alone, for-homecoming longing and for-his-wife,
the-nymph queenly was-holding-back, Calypso, shining-one among-goddesses,
in caves hollow, yearning her-husband that-he-be.
But when now the-year came, as-rolled-round the-seasons,
in-which for-him spun-the-thread the-gods homeward to-return
to Ithaca, not-even there escaped was-he from-trials,
even among his-own dear-ones. The-gods but pitied-him all
apart-from Poseidon; he but unceasingly raged
literal: Tell me of the man, Muse, the man of many turns, who very many
ways was driven wandering, after he sacked the sacred citadel of Troy;
he saw the cities of many men and came to know their mind,
and many were the griefs he suffered at sea in his heart,
striving to win his own life and the homecoming of his comrades.
But not even so did he save his comrades, though he longed to;
for by their own recklessness they perished,
fools, who devoured the cattle of Hyperion the Sun;
and he took from them the day of their homecoming.
Of these things, from whatever point, goddess, daughter of Zeus, tell us also.
Then all the others, as many as had fled sheer destruction,
were at home, having escaped both the war and the sea;
but him alone, longing for his homecoming and his wife,
the queenly nymph held back — Calypso, shining among goddesses —
in her hollow caves, yearning that he be her husband.
But when at last the year came, as the seasons rolled round,
in which the gods had spun for him to return home
to Ithaca, not even there was he free of trials,
even among his own people. And all the gods pitied him
except Poseidon; he raged without ceasing
translations.natural:
Sing me the man, Muse — that man of many turns
who wandered far and long once he had sacked
the sacred height of Troy. He saw the cities
of many peoples and he learned their minds,
and the sea gave him grief on grief at heart,
fighting for his life and his men's way home.
He could not save them, want it as he might:
their own blind recklessness destroyed them all —
the fools — they ate the cattle of the Sun,
and the Sun blotted out their homecoming day.
Begin wherever you will, goddess, daughter of Zeus,
and tell the story over again for us.

By now the rest, all those who had escaped
the steep pitch of destruction, were at home,
done with the war and done with the sea. Him only,
aching for home and wife, the queenly nymph
Calypso, radiant among goddesses, held
in her hollow caves, hungry to make him her husband.
Even when the wheeling seasons brought the year
the gods had spun for his return to Ithaca,
he was not clear of struggle, even there,
even among his own. The gods all pitied him —
all but Poseidon, who raged on, unappeased.

free.homeric:
Sing in me, Muse, of the man of many ways, the wanderer,
hurled across the world after he plundered Troy's holy citadel.
Many the cities of men he saw, many the minds he came to know,
and many the sorrows that broke on his heart across the open sea
as he fought to bring his own life home, and his companions with it.
Yet them he could not save, though all his soul desired it:
they died of their own headlong folly, the fools,
who slaughtered and ate the cattle of Helios, lord Sun —
and the god struck the day of homecoming from their lives forever.
From some point here, goddess, daughter of Zeus, take up the tale for us.

Now all the rest who had fled steep destruction
were home at last from the war and the sea.
One man alone still hungered for his homecoming and his wife —
and him the queenly nymph Calypso, brightest of goddesses,
held in her arching caverns, burning to make him her husband.
And when the circling seasons brought round the very year
the gods had spun for his return to his own hall in Ithaca,
not even then, not even among his people, was he past his trials.
All the gods looked on him with pity —
all but Poseidon, whose wrath ran on unbroken.
free.shakespearean:
Speak, Muse, of that much-turnèd wanderer
Who, having razed the sacred towers of Troy,
Was driven long and far upon the deep.
Full many were the towns of men he saw,
And many minds of men he came to know;
And many woes he suffered on the sea,
In striving for his life and comrades' home.
Yet them he saved not, though he wished it sore,
For by their own mad folly did they die:
The fools! that slew and ate the Sun-god's kine,
Whereat the god struck out their homeward day.
Of these things, goddess, child of thund'ring Jove,
Begin where't please thee, and to us recount.

Now all the rest that fled from steep-down death
Were safe at home, from war and wave escaped;
Him only, longing for his wife and home,
The nymph Calypso, queenliest of her kind,
Kept close within her hollow-vaulted caves,
And yearned to bind him to her as her lord.
But when the wheeling seasons brought the year
The gods had spun for his return at last
To Ithaca, not even there his toils
Were done, not even midst his kith and kin.
The gods did pity him, save one alone:
Poseidon, raging without stint or stay.
free.modernist:
Tell me about the man, Muse. The man who was never at a loss. He sacked the holy city of Troy and then he wandered a long time. He saw many cities and he learned how men thought. At sea he took his beatings in his heart. He was trying to get his own life back and to get his men home.

It did not work. Not for the men. They died because of what they did. They were fools. They ate the cattle of the Sun. The Sun saw to it that they never got home. Start anywhere, goddess. Tell it to us too.

The others who had lived through the war were home now. The war was done and the sea was done. One man was not home. He wanted his home and he wanted his wife. The nymph Calypso had him in her caves. She was a fine goddess and she wanted him for a husband. The seasons went around and the year came that the gods had set for him to go home to Ithaca. Even then it was not over. Even with his own people it was not over. The gods were sorry for him. All but Poseidon. Poseidon stayed angry.
free.lucretian:
Consider now, my student, what a man is: this one, resourceful past
all others, whom we ask the Muse to summon — for song itself is motion
of thin air, and memory the trace of things impressed upon the mind.
When Troy's high citadel — mere stone and timber, though men called it
sacred — was brought down, he wandered; and in wandering, observed:
how many cities, and in each the minds of men, their customs and their
errors, from which causes may be learned. Upon the barren sea — that
restless plain of matter never still — he suffered much, seeking to
preserve that fragile compound, life, both in himself and in his
comrades. Yet mark this well, for it instructs: they perished not by
fate, nor by the anger of the void, but by their own unreason, having
eaten of the cattle of the Sun — for appetite unruled destroys the very
frame that houses it — and so the light of homecoming was taken from
them utterly. From any point you choose, O goddess (if goddess there
must be), unwind the tale for us.

The rest, who had escaped destruction's edge, sat quiet at their
hearths, the war concluded and the sea crossed over. One man alone —
still craving home and wife, those bonds by which our mortal nature
is composed — the nymph Calypso held within her hollow caves, desiring
him for husband; for desire compels the goddess no less than the
mortal frame. And when the circling years — that endless wheel of
seasons, matter's calendar — brought round the term the gods had spun
for his return to Ithaca, not even then was struggle done, not even
among his own. The gods felt pity for him, all but one: the lord of
seas, Poseidon, in whom anger, like a charge long stored, ran on
unspent.
free.storybook:
Once upon a time, dear reader, there was a clever, clever man — the cleverest man in all the world. Tell us about him, Muse! After he burned the beautiful city of Troy right down to the ground, he wandered ever so far across the sea. He visited many wonderful cities and met many interesting people, and oh, the things he learned! But the sea made his heart very sad. He tried so hard to keep himself alive and to bring his friends safely home.

But do you know what? His friends did not come home. Not a single one. They did a very foolish thing: they ate the pretty cows that belonged to the Sun. And so the Sun made sure that every last one of them died before they ever saw home again. Start wherever you like, goddess, and tell us the story too.

Now, all the other soldiers who had not been killed were snug at home, finished with the war and the sea. All except one man. He missed his home very much, and he missed his wife too. A beautiful fairy named Calypso was keeping him in her cave, because she wanted him for her very own husband — whether he liked it or not. And even when the year finally came round, the year the gods had promised he could go home to Ithaca, his troubles were not over. Not even close, not even with his own family. All the gods felt sorry for him. All except Poseidon, the god of the sea. Poseidon stayed angry, and angry, and angry.

murray.text: Tell me, O Muse, of the man of many devices, who wandered full many ways after he had sacked the sacred citadel of Troy. Many were the men whose cities he saw and whose mind he learned, aye, and many the woes he suffered in his heart upon the sea,

seeking to win his own life and the return of his comrades. Yet even so he saved not his comrades, though he desired it sore, for through their own blind folly they perished—fools, who devoured the kine of Helios Hyperion; but he took from them the day of their returning.

Of these things, goddess, daughter of Zeus, beginning where thou wilt, tell thou even unto us. Now all the rest, as many as had escaped sheer destruction, were at home, safe from both war and sea, but Odysseus alone, filled with longing for his return and for his wife, did the queenly nymph Calypso, that bright goddess,

keep back in her hollow caves, yearning that he should be her husband. But when, as the seasons revolved, the year came in which the gods had ordained that he should return home to Ithaca, not even there was he free from toils, even among his own folk. And all the gods pitied him

save Poseidon; but he continued to rage unceasingly against godlike Odysseus until at length he reached his own land. Howbeit Poseidon had gone among the far-off Ethiopians—the Ethiopians who dwell sundered in twain, the farthermost of men, some where Hyperion sets and some where he rises,
